hero.headline_a_1: The data does not exist in the field before the encounter.
hero.sub1_a: When you write "access barrier" next to a passage, you are not just interpreting what your interlocutor said. You are constructing one possible version of an encounter that could have produced "adaptive pragmatism", "veiled resistance", or a concept that does not yet have a name.
hero.sub2_a: TRAMA was built for researchers who know that fieldwork does not precede analysis. And who want a tool that does not pretend otherwise.
hero.sub1_b: TRAMA is a qualitative coding platform built on a simple and consequential premise: there is no neutral interpretation. Coding is one of the ways to analyze information from the field. It is the act of naming interview passages, grouping text fragments into patterns, and assigning categories to the collected material. This process is often presented as a technical extraction procedure, as if the themes were already in the data waiting to be discovered. TRAMA assumes the opposite: all coding is interpretation, and all interpretation is the act of a subject situated in time, space, and the power relations that cross their research. Instead of simulating an objectivity that conceals the researcher, the app creates space for them to record their choices, make their framings explicit, and take epistemic responsibility for the map they construct.
hero.microcopy: No AI · your data stays with you · AGPL-3.0
hero.differential_free_desc: Free plan. Upgrade when you need.

how_it_works.headline: From transcript to insight, in one place.
how_it_works.step1_desc: Paste a transcript, upload a file. Your data never leaves the server.
how_it_works.step2_desc: Select passages and assign codes you create. You choose the names — and that choice is already an interpretation.
how_it_works.step3_desc: Group codes into themes. Themes are not categories — they are answers to your research questions.
how_it_works.step4_desc: Explore the web of relations between themes, documents and codes. Export SVG, PNG or a narrative report.

political_section.headline: Every analytical tool presupposes a theory of data.
political_section.body1: Most tools presuppose that data exists before you arrive, that analysis comes after fieldwork, and that interpretation is a matter of finding what was already there. This presupposition is wrong, and the consequences of accepting it without examination show up in the results.
political_section.body2: Data is co-produced in the encounter between researcher, interlocutor, and context. The separation between data collection and analysis is a convenient methodological fiction, not a description of what actually happens. And the tool you use to organize your analysis already carries a theory of what data is, even if it never says so.
political_section.concept1_title: Data does not precede the encounter
political_section.concept1_body: Recording, transcribing, and coding are all acts of production, not capture. What you name "access barrier" is already the result of a process that began before the transcript and continues after it. A tool that treats data as a stable object falsifies this process.
political_section.concept2_title: Every interpretation is one possible version, not the correct one
political_section.concept2_body: Naming a passage is constructing one reading among other legitimate readings. The validity of an analysis does not come from eliminating other possible readings, but from making explicit why this reading, made from this position, with these questions. Reflexivity is not confession — it is method.
political_section.concept3_title: The tool is not neutral, and pretending it is comes at a cost
political_section.concept3_body: An interface that organizes data in hierarchies already suggests that knowledge is hierarchical. A system that uses AI to suggest categories has already decided that categories exist before the encounter. These are not technical choices — they are epistemological positions inscribed in code. TRAMA refuses this opacity.
political_section.blockquote: "You do not find the data. You produce it: in the field, in the transcript, in the reading. A tool that does not recognize this is not neutral — it is naive."
political_section.blockquote_source: From the article Coding is Interpreting

final_cta.headline: Start your analysis today.
final_cta.sub: Free. Your data stays with you.

dashboard.banner_body: Take the introduction tour to understand how reflexive analysis works.
dashboard.empty_body: Create your first project to start organizing your interviews and analyses.
tips: You can code the same passage with multiple codes — reality is multidimensional.
Descriptive names work better than evaluative ones. "Mentions cost" is more useful than "Complains about price".
Themes are not categories — they are answers to your research questions.
Memos are part of the analysis, not the margin of the data. Record your interpretive reasoning.
Import all documents before coding — patterns are easier to see with the full material.
The code you choose is already an interpretation. There is no neutral code.
Reflexivity is not confession — it is method. Record why you named it that way.
The web shows connections that linear text hides. Use it to review your analysis.
Renaming a code changes the framing of all passages marked with it.
Two researchers reading the same passage can code it differently — and both can be right.

onboarding.slide1_intro: You read an interview and find:
onboarding.slide1_quote: "The price is high, but the quality is worth it."
onboarding.slide1_body: You could name this passage in three ways:
onboarding.slide1_conclusion: Each name is one possible version. The code you choose is already an interpretation — there is no neutral code.
onboarding.slide2_body: After coding, you will notice that some codes speak to related things.
onboarding.slide2_conclusion: Themes are not categories — they are answers to your research questions.
onboarding.slide3_body: With themes and documents, TRAMA shows how they connect in an interactive visual network.
onboarding.slide3_conclusion: TRAMA does not interpret the web — you do. The tool organizes what you produced in the encounter with the data.

templates.title: New Project
templates.subtitle: Choose a starting point for your analysis

workspace.no_docs_body: Paste a transcribed interview, upload a file, or start with our example. TRAMA supports TXT, MD, PDF and DOCX.
workspace.no_docs_tip: Tip: import all documents before coding — patterns are easier to spot with the full material.
workspace.no_code_body: The name you choose is already an interpretation. There is no neutral code.
workspace.no_theme_body: Themes are not categories — they are answers to your research questions.

export.title: Export Analysis
export.format_pdf_full_desc: Cover · Executive Summary · Theme Analysis · Methodology
export.format_pdf_themes_desc: Compact version — no detailed quotes, ideal for presentations
export.format_csv_desc: Flat table: Theme | Code | Document | Quote | Date
export.format_md_desc: Structured with headings — version-controllable in Git, editable in any editor
export.summary: The report will include: {documents} documents · {codes} codes · {themes} themes

tooltips.new_code_simple: Create a code to categorize passages
tooltips.new_code_deep: The name you choose is already an interpretation. Descriptive names, not evaluative ones.
tooltips.new_theme_deep: Themes are answers to research questions, not groupings by superficial similarity.
tooltips.export_deep: The report includes reflexive methodology: where you were, not just what you found.
tooltips.memo_simple: Add a note to the coding
tooltips.memo_deep: Memos document your interpretive reasoning. They are part of the analysis, not the margin of the data.
tooltips.search_simple: Global search across docs, codes and themes
tooltips.search_deep: Searching across the whole analysis at once shows how your interpretations connect.

admin.delete_confirm_body: This action is permanent. All projects, documents, codes and themes of this user will be deleted.
